cross_connections.display_label: First cousins, sons of brothers Job and Samuel Stiles
Both Hooker descendants died in Waterville Lucas County Ohio

narrative_blocks.header: Migrated to Waterville Lucas County Ohio
narrative_blocks.body: Hylas Stiles was born in Windsor CT (1793, same day-of-year as his sister Laura), grew up through the family's Enfield CT period, and migrated to Waterville in Lucas County, Ohio at some point before his 1871 death. The migration places him in the Connecticut-to-Ohio settlement pattern that brought several Hooker descendants westward — his second cousin Leonard Tuttle settled in Medina County Ohio by 1836, his first cousin Henry Stiles also died in Ohio, and Hooker descendant Nathaniel Eggleston Hooker died in the same Waterville OH fifteen years after Hylas.

narrative_blocks.header: One of three Stiles cousins to migrate Ohio
narrative_blocks.body: Hylas Stiles was one of three Esther Hooker Stiles descendants to migrate to Ohio in the same generation. His first cousin Henry Stiles(son of his uncle Samuel) also died in Ohio. His second cousin Leonard Tuttle(grandson of his great-aunt Keziah Stiles via Kezia Munson Tuttle) settled in Medina County Ohio by 1836.

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entry 1206
Henry Reed Stiles, The Stiles Family in America (1895), entry 416

career.notes: Shoemaker by trade (EH 462 + Stiles book). The Stiles family resided in Enfield CT for about ten years (c.1810-1821) where Job died in 1813, then his widow returned to Windsor.

burial.plot_notes: Palisado Cemetery, Windsor CT — buried 'on one stone' with wife Mary Drake Stiles.

narrative_blocks.header: Windsor shoemaker, brother of silversmith Samuel
narrative_blocks.body: Job Stiles was a shoemaker by trade, working in Windsor CT and later Enfield CT. His older brother Samuel Stiles was a Northampton silversmith and clockmaker — together they represent two New England craft trades within the same Stiles family generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at forty-eight, buried with Mary in Windsor
narrative_blocks.body: Job died at Windsor CT on April 13, 1813 at age 48, during the family's roughly decade-long Enfield CT residence (c.1810-1821). His widow Mary Drake Stiles survived him by twenty-six years, dying March 14, 1839 at age 77. They were buried together in the Old Windsor Graveyard with their names inscribed on a single stone.

burial.plot_notes: Palisado Cemetery, Windsor CT — 'on one stone' with husband Job Stiles.

parents.father_research_notes: Of Windsor CT. Not in JSON.
parents.mother_research_notes: Of Windsor CT, of the Griswold family — middle name of grandson Ezra Griswold Stiles (HD4158) carries this lineage. Not in JSON.

narrative_blocks.header: Twenty-six years widowed, buried three children
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Drake Stiles was widowed at fifty-one in 1813 when her husband Job died. She lived another twenty-six years in Windsor, dying at seventy-seven in 1839. She buried three of her nine children: infant Mary in September 1786 (age 5 months), Laura in September 1810 (age 21), and Ezra Griswold lost at sea around 1819 (presumed dead at 28).

sources.label: Edward Hooker, Descendants of Rev. Thomas Hooker (1909), entry 462
Henry Reed Stiles, The Stiles Family in America (1895)

military_service.notes: Per The Stiles Family in America (BIB008) entry #230: served in the Revolution and was at Horse Neck from May 1781 to March 1782. Horse Neck (Greenwich CT) was an active Revolutionary War outpost under occasional British raid throughout the war.
Served in the Revolution at Horse Neck (now Greenwich CT, on the western frontier facing British-held New York), from May 1781 to March 1782.

narrative_blocks.header: Inherited mansion, lost all endorsing for friend
narrative_blocks.body: Ashbel Stiles inherited the family mansion and a very comfortable property from his father in North Haven Connecticut. But in an evil hour he endorsed for a friend and lost his all. Soon after he removed to Windsor Connecticut, and thence to Norwich Massachusetts — now Huntington — near his daughter Ruth Stiles Ellis.

narrative_blocks.header: Hannah died Norwich one month before Ashbel did
narrative_blocks.body: After fifty-one years of marriage, Ashbel and his cousin-wife Hannah Stiles both died at Norwich (now Huntington) Massachusetts in autumn 1810 — both at age seventy-five. Hannah went first in September; Ashbel followed in October, approximately one month later.

narrative_blocks.header: Son thrown from a horse, killed in Jamaica
narrative_blocks.body: His son Benjamin Stiles died at Jamaica in the West Indies from injuries sustained being thrown from a horse. The Stiles Family in America entry 230 records the death without further circumstance — a son lost an ocean from home to a single fall.

narrative_blocks.header: Horse Neck garrison 1781 after financial ruin
narrative_blocks.body: Forty-six at enlistment, he came to Revolutionary militia service well after most of his cohort. Financial ruin from endorsing for a friend had already emptied the family mansion he had inherited from his father.

parents.father_research_notes: Lt. Samuel Stiles of Windsor CT

bio.notes: Hannah Stiles married her cousin Ashbell Stiles. She was daughter of Lt. Samuel Stiles and Mary Phelps of Windsor CT. Same surname before and after marriage.
